notes: meta and rendered maps are populated in the loop but never returned or otherwise used in either Python input or Go output; computed variables silently discarded. Consistent with the truncated Python snippet but worth a reviewer confirming the full method is elsewhere.

Python input field contains Go syntax artifacts: 'strings.Join(block_parts, separator)' and 'merged = append(merged, para)' appear verbatim inside the Python function body. The input is corrupted with Go-language markers.

Near-identical to ID 313: _split_at_list_items and _split_at_table_rows have exactly the same logic, the same transformation rules applied, and produce outputs that differ only in function name. The Go outputs are character-for-character identical after substituting ListItems/TableRows. One of the two should be dropped.
Near-identical to ID 312: same logic, same rules, outputs differ only in function name. Reject one.
Input is 9 lines (< 10 lines threshold) with a single nested if block. Transformation applies only R09 (negative slice index) and R11 (isspace check). Too simple for 14B tier.
Input is only 5 lines with a single if branch. Trivially simple transformation for 14B tier.

Python uses lazy per-message iteration (try/except per message); Go converts to eager pre-load of all messages before iterating. Behavioral drift for large mailboxes. Reviewer should confirm semantics are acceptable.

Python input contains Go syntax artifact 'detail := chunk.source_detail' (walrus-style assignment). Output drops the dict-vs-list dispatch (isinstance(queries, dict)) entirely: Go signature accepts only [][2]string, losing the dict-keyed query map branch that the instruction cites R11 for. D2=1 (silent behavior drop of dict handling). D3=1 (Go marker in Python input field). D4=2 (R11 cited but type switch not implemented).

Input is 12 lines with a single if count != 0 check. The transformation applies R03/R07/R11 but the logic is purely mechanical (SQL exec + rowcount check). Borderline D5=2: minimal branching, mechanical transform.

Input is 11 lines of purely mechanical string-prefix dispatch with no type conversion, no error handling, no struct/method transformation. Only two rules cited (R01, R11). Transformation is trivially mechanical rename + strings.HasPrefix substitution with no branching complexity warranting 14B tier.
Output uses bytes.NewReader throughout but the import block only lists fmt plus arrow/parquet packages; bytes import is missing. One fixable omission in a non-critical path.

Python input has a stray closing brace on the last line of the for-loop body (line 870: `}`), making the input not valid Python. The instruction is still actionable and the Go output is correct, but the input field contains a syntax artifact that could confuse a training model.

Near-duplicate: compound input contains the same two functions (cluster_entities and CooccurrenceMatrix.build) that are independently translated in IDs 343 and 344 respectively. The output uses plain function parameters instead of the options struct approach from 343, introducing an inconsistency. No new transformation pattern is introduced. D6=1 (same transformation of same functions already in batch). D3=2 because the compound framing is ambiguous given the conflicting signature style.

Near-duplicate of ID 345 (BuildClusterMap factory) and ID 350 (same ClusterMap struct + BuildClusterMap). The __init__ adds the NewClusterMap constructor which is new, but the factory BuildClusterMap and struct definition are essentially identical to 345 and 350. D6=2 rather than 1 because the constructor portion adds incremental teaching value.

Four trivial one-liner accessor methods: a map lookup with nil return, a map-values-to-slice loop, and two len() calls. No statement-level branching beyond a single nil guard. The combined input is only 12 lines of pure field access/delegation with no logic complexity. Fails D5 complexity threshold.

The Build method is substantially identical to ID 344 (same nested pair-counting loop and scoring logic). The constructor is new teaching material. D6=2 because the combined example overlaps heavily with 344 — only the NewCooccurrenceMatrix constructor adds distinct value.

Near-duplicate: the ClusterMap struct definition and BuildClusterMap factory are literally the same code as ID 347, and the factory pattern is also covered by ID 345. GetNeighbors adds a small new method but does not justify repeating the full struct + factory. D6=1 due to the struct and factory being identical to 347.

to_dict is the only transformed function, and it is reproduced verbatim inside ID 354 (same class, same function body). D5=2: 10+ lines but only a single dict comprehension, no statement-level loop inside the method body itself. D6=1: same function name, same class, essentially identical transformation already present in ID 354.
Input has only 8 non-empty lines (< 10 threshold). Single for loop with one if/continue. Too short for 14B tier.

The clusterEntities function in the output is character-for-character identical to the clusterEntities output in ID 351. D6=2 (near-identical sub-component reuse); however the compound context adds constructor+build patterns not seen in 351, so Accept.

Python stores thread handle (self._thread = t); Go goroutine has no handle and the struct field is dropped. Accepted Go idiom but a reviewer should confirm the missing join/cancel capability is intentional.

Python _user_template uses .format(topic_label=..., n_chunks=..., chunks=...) with named kwargs. Go output uses fmt.Sprintf(cs.userTemplate, ...) with positional %s/%d args. If the stored template uses {name} placeholders (Python default), fmt.Sprintf will not substitute correctly. Instruction cites R08 (f-string) but the template is a stored string field, not an f-string — ambiguous whether template format was also converted. D2=2 (behavioral drift reviewers should confirm), D3=2 (ambiguity about template format handling).

Python INPUT field contains Go artifacts: 'sc.embedding = nil' (nil is a Go keyword, Python would use None), 'priorities[i]' (variable never defined in the given snippet), 'sel_mat' (also undefined). The input is corrupted/incomplete Python code making it impossible to derive correct output from instruction+input alone. D3=1 (input contradicts itself — Go keywords in Python code). D2=2 because the Go output resolves priorities via s.getPriority(candidates[i].Provenance), which is a new method not implied by the undefined priorities[] array in the input.
Input is only 7 non-empty lines (< 10 threshold). Single type-check guard plus a list comprehension converted to a loop. Too simple for 14B tier.
10 lines exactly; type switch is non-trivial transformation but only a single top-level switch with minimal branching inside each case.

D2=2: phases and conditions stored as []interface{} rather than []string, a minor type drift from the Python list[str]. D6=2: same annotate-chunk pattern (build meta map, regex search, section fallback, set source_detail) as ID 376; different domain but near-identical transformation structure.

D6=2: same annotate-chunk pattern as 376 and 380 (build meta/rendered maps, regex search in content, section fallback, set source_detail/rendered_source). Detail filtering of None values is a minor variation but does not constitute a distinct transformation pattern.
D6=2: core annotate-chunk pattern identical to 376/380/386. The FindStringSubmatch/group(1) extraction is a minor variant, not a new transformation pattern.

D3=1: INPUT field contains Go syntax artifacts — `strings.Join(header_lines, "\n")` and `parts = append(parts, attachment_blocks...)` appear in the supposedly-Python input. This corrupts the training signal; a model cannot learn the Python→Go transformation from a contaminated input. D4=2: cited rules include all Go constructs which are already partially present in the input.

D2=1: `collectParts(mr)` is called inside the outer `for _, preferredType := range` loop, consuming the multipart reader on the first iteration. On the second iteration (text/html fallback), the reader is already exhausted so parts will be empty. The Python `msg.walk()` re-walks all parts for each preferred type. The Go output silently drops the html fallback path.

D4=2: instruction cites R10 (for-range over decoded pairs) but the output has no for loop — it delegates entirely to `mime.WordDecoder.DecodeHeader`. The rule is cited with a visible input instance but is not applied in the output. D5=2: 13 input lines with a single if + a for loop, but the transformation is purely mechanical (empty-check guard + library call); both minimal branching AND trivially mechanical apply.

D5=2: 13 input lines; can_render is a short loop with index cap, render is a one-liner generator. Together they are mostly mechanical with only a single meaningful loop. D6=2: can_render is near-identical in structure to ID 379 (iterStudies loop with limit cap, return true on key presence); render is a trivial join loop seen in multiple other render methods.

D3=1: INPUT field contains Go syntax artifact — `matched_key := ""` (short variable declaration with `:=`) embedded in the Python code body. This is invalid Python and corrupts the training example. D4=2: the contaminated input makes rule-citation verification unreliable for the affected lines.

D6=2: iterLabels uses the same type-switch dispatch pattern as iterStudies (ID 377); the labelID function adds a novel fallback chain but the primary iter function is near-duplicate. Scores 2 rather than 1 because labelID teaches a distinct multi-level fallback pattern.

D6=2: same annotate-chunk structure as 376/380/386/388 (build meta/rendered maps, regex FindString in content, section loop, set SourceDetail/RenderedSource). The rtype/rid key format is a small variation, not a new transformation pattern.

D5=1: input is only 9 lines — below the 10-line threshold. D6=2: can_render structure is nearly identical to ID 379 (ClinicalTrialRenderer.can_render): check empty list, loop up to 3 items, check key presence, return true/false.
